executive_summary.body:
GlucoTrack Lite is a mobile application intended to support adults with diabetes by logging self-measured blood glucose readings and delivering rule-based or AI-driven lifestyle nudges relating to diet, physical activity, and adherence behaviours. The product is positioned at the boundary between a general wellness tool and Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under the evolving CDSCO SaMD framework (October 2025 draft) and the IMDRF risk categorisation model. Based on a preliminary mapping, the application appears to provide information that may influence clinical management of a non-serious chronic condition, which typically aligns with IMDRF Category II and CDSCO Class B SaMD. The classification, however, is not yet formally resolved and is the most material open question for this submission pack.

The likely regulatory pathway is a domestic manufacturing licence application via Form MD-5 to the State Licensing Authority for a Class B SaMD, supported by a documented Quality Management System aligned with ISO 13485:2016 and a software lifecycle process aligned with IEC 62304. If clinical validation is undertaken in India to substantiate nudge performance, a Form MD-12 test licence and Ethics Committee approval may also be triggered. If the nudge claims are subsequently scoped upward to influence treatment decisions for a serious profile, the pathway may shift to Class C and the Central Licensing Authority (Form MD-7), with associated clinical investigation expectations.

The current readiness profile is at the floor: no QMS is in place, no clinical or analytical validation data has been generated, and no prior CDSCO engagement is recorded. The risk exposure is assessed as medium rather than high because the application is presently passive-display plus lifestyle-nudge in nature, not a closed-loop therapy driver. Resolving classification, initiating QMS build, and designing a pilot validation study are the immediate priorities before any CDSCO submission activity is initiated.
executive_summary.product_class: Likely Class B SaMD (boundary case — classification to be formally confirmed)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — Form MD-5 application to the CDSCO State Licensing Authority for a Class B SaMD, with Form MD-12 test licence likely if a clinical validation study is conducted in India
headline_gaps: Resolve the wellness-vs-SaMD classification boundary through a formal IMDRF mapping and written rationale before finalising marketing claims.
Initiate an ISO 13485:2016 and IEC 62304 gap assessment and begin QMS build, as no quality system is currently in place.
Design and register a pilot clinical validation study on CTRI with Ethics Committee approval to generate performance evidence for the nudge algorithm.

intended_use.indication: GlucoTrack Lite is intended to support self-management of diabetes mellitus in adults by enabling users to log self-measured blood glucose readings and receive lifestyle guidance relating to diet, physical activity, medication adherence reminders, and behavioural patterns. The application is intended as an adjunct to, and not a replacement for, professional medical care, prescribed therapy, or clinically validated glucose-monitoring devices.
intended_use.intended_user: The primary intended user is an adult lay person with a self-reported or clinically diagnosed condition of diabetes mellitus (Type 1 or Type 2) who is capable of operating a smartphone application and entering glucose values either manually or via a connected meter. Caregivers of such individuals may also use the application on behalf of the patient. The application is not intended for use by healthcare professionals as a clinical decision-support tool in a hospital or clinic environment.
intended_use.use_environment: The application is intended for use in a home or community setting on a personal smartphone running supported versions of Android or iOS. It is not intended for use in critical-care, emergency, peri-operative, or in-patient clinical environments. Internet connectivity is typically required for synchronisation, nudge delivery, and consent management functions.
intended_use.contraindications: The application is not intended for use in paediatric populations, pregnant women with gestational diabetes requiring active titration of insulin, individuals with hypoglycaemia unawareness, or patients in acute metabolic decompensation. It is not intended to direct insulin dosing, medication titration, or any closed-loop therapeutic action. Users experiencing symptoms of severe hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia are directed to contact a registered medical practitioner or emergency services.

device_description.components_architecture: GlucoTrack Lite is a standalone software product comprising a mobile front-end (Android and iOS applications), a cloud back-end hosting user accounts and logged data, and a rules-and-machine-learning layer that generates lifestyle nudges based on logged glucose values and behavioural inputs. The system is expected to include user authentication, a local data store, secure transport to the cloud, a notification service for nudges, and an administrative console for clinical and engineering oversight. No proprietary hardware accessory is bundled at this stage; integration with third-party glucose meters via Bluetooth may be offered as an optional pathway in a future release.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The user enters or imports a glucose reading along with optional contextual inputs (meals, activity, mood, medication). The application stores the reading, presents trends and summaries, and applies a configurable rule set, optionally augmented by a machine-learning model, to surface lifestyle nudges relating to dietary choices, activity patterns, and adherence behaviours. The application does not compute insulin doses, does not modify therapy, and does not issue alarms intended to drive immediate clinical action. All nudges are framed as informational guidance to support self-management.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only product, no biological, chemical, or patient-contacting materials are involved. The product is expected to be developed and maintained in conformity with ISO 13485:2016 (quality management), IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), and ISO/IEC 27001 and the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 for information security and data protection. Cybersecurity controls are expected to align with applicable CDSCO and IMDRF cybersecurity guidance.
device_description.variants_accessories: A single functional variant is currently planned, distributed through the Google Play Store and Apple App Store. Optional accessory pathways may include connectivity to commercially available, separately regulated glucose meters; such meters are not part of this submission. No physical accessories, consumables, or sterile components are associated with the product.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software updates are expected to be released through the respective app stores under a controlled change-management process governed by the QMS. End-of-life management includes orderly user notification, export of personal data on request, and secure deletion of user data in line with DPDP obligations. There is no physical disposal or environmental waste consideration applicable to the product.

risk_classification.imdrf_significance: Inform clinical management (boundary case — may shift to Drive clinical management if nudge claims are scoped upward)
risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Diabetes self-management in stable, ambulatory adults is typically considered a non-serious healthcare situation under the IMDRF SaMD framework, as the immediate consequences of an erroneous lifestyle nudge are unlikely to result in death or irreversible harm. The application provides information that supports user awareness of glucose patterns and lifestyle choices, which maps to the Inform-clinical-management significance level rather than Drive or Diagnose/Treat. The intersection of Non-serious situation and Inform significance corresponds to IMDRF Category II. This mapping is conditional on the final framing of nudge claims; if the claims evolve to recommend specific therapy adjustments, the significance may rise and the category may shift to III.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, software providing information intended to support clinical management of a non-serious chronic condition typically aligns with low-moderate risk Class B. This is consistent with the IMDRF Category II mapping above. The Class B call assumes that lifestyle nudges remain framed as general guidance for self-management and do not drive insulin titration, medication changes, or immediate clinical action. The Class B pathway is expected to proceed to the State Licensing Authority via Form MD-5. The classification call is preliminary and is expected to be confirmed through a formal regulatory strategy document and, where appropriate, a CDSCO pre-submission consultation prior to filing.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Diabetes mellitus affects an estimated 100 million adults in India, with self-management adherence and lifestyle behaviour widely recognised as principal determinants of long-term glycaemic outcomes. Digital tools that consolidate glucose self-monitoring with structured behavioural nudges may support sustained engagement, improve recording discipline, and complement periodic clinical review by registered medical practitioners. The product addresses the gap between episodic clinic visits and day-to-day self-management decisions, without substituting for prescribed therapy or clinically validated diagnostic devices.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: Comparable digital diabetes self-management applications are available internationally, including products such as mySugr (Roche) and Dario Health's diabetes management platform, both of which have been positioned as low-to-moderate risk SaMD or general wellness products in their respective jurisdictions depending on claim scope. Within India, the regulatory predicate landscape for SaMD is still maturing under the October 2025 CDSCO draft, and direct CDSCO-licensed predicates may be limited. A predicate analysis is expected to be documented as part of the regulatory strategy.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The evidence plan is expected to include analytical validation of the nudge logic against curated glucose datasets, a usability evaluation aligned with IEC 62366-1, and a prospective pilot clinical validation study conducted with at least one clinical partner in India to assess engagement, adherence, and self-management outcome surrogates. The pilot is expected to be registered on the Clinical Trials Registry of India (CTRI) and to obtain prior Ethics Committee approval consistent with ICMR ethical guidelines. Outcomes from the pilot are expected to inform labelling, the Instructions for Use, and any future scope expansion of nudge claims.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is expected to be drafted to describe the scope of permitted future changes to the nudge algorithm, including periodic retraining on accumulated user data, refinement of rule thresholds based on observed engagement and outcomes, and addition of new behavioural input categories. The PCCP is expected to define the boundaries within which changes may be implemented under the existing licence without triggering a fresh submission, consistent with emerging CDSCO and IMDRF expectations for adaptive SaMD.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol is expected to specify, for each anticipated modification type, the data sources used for retraining, the validation datasets and acceptance criteria applied, the verification and regression testing performed, the documentation maintained in the Design History File, and the internal review and approval gates. Material changes that fall outside the PCCP envelope — such as scope expansion to drive clinical management, addition of paediatric users, or therapy-titration features — are expected to be handled as a fresh regulatory submission rather than under the protocol.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world monitoring is expected to include tracking of nudge delivery and dismissal rates, user-reported adverse events and complaints, periodic algorithm performance metrics against held-out and emerging datasets, and a structured signal-detection process for unintended behavioural or clinical effects. Findings are expected to be reviewed at a defined cadence by a cross-functional team and reported in line with CDSCO post-market surveillance and Periodic Safety Update Report obligations applicable to the assigned device class.

executive_summary.body:
GlucoTrack Lite is a mobile application designed to assist individuals with diabetes by logging glucose readings and delivering rule-based or AI-driven lifestyle nudges. Based on the product description and the readiness analysis, the application likely qualifies as a Class B Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under India's Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (MDR 2017), as interpreted through the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance. This classification is conditional on how the lifestyle nudge outputs are framed: nudges positioned as general wellness prompts may remain outside SaMD scope, whereas nudges framed as condition-specific clinical guidance for diabetes self-management are expected to trigger a Class B SaMD designation under the IMDRF non-serious healthcare situation combined with a 'drive clinical management' significance profile.

The regulatory pathway most likely applicable is a state-authority manufacturing licence under MD-5 (for a domestically developed software product), with the Central Licensing Authority potentially involved if classification escalates to Class C due to clinical-claim expansion. An MD-12 test licence may be required if a formal clinical investigation is initiated to validate the nudge algorithm's performance.

The product is currently at readiness band Red (2 out of 10), reflecting the absence of a Quality Management System, no clinical or analytical validation evidence, and no formal CDSCO engagement to date. Risk exposure is assessed as medium — the passive logging and nudge architecture does not constitute a closed-loop treatment system, but the boundary between wellness and SaMD is thin and actively evolving under CDSCO guidance. Founders are advised to resolve the classification question, initiate an ISO 13485 gap assessment, and develop clinical validation evidence before any marketing claims are finalised or CDSCO engagement is pursued.
executive_summary.product_class: Class B SaMD (conditional — subject to formal classification analysis of nudge claim specificity)
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic software development — MD-5 application to the State Licensing Authority for Class B SaMD; MD-12 test licence likely required if clinical investigation is conducted to validate the nudge algorithm; Central Licensing Authority pathway applicable if classification escalates to Class C
headline_gaps: Resolve SaMD vs. wellness classification boundary by commissioning a formal written classification rationale against Oct 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance before finalising any marketing claims.
Initiate an ISO 13485:2016 and IEC 62304 gap assessment immediately — a documented QMS is a prerequisite for CDSCO submission and typically requires 6–9 months to build from scratch.
Design and CTRI-register a pilot clinical validation study for the nudge algorithm, with Ethics Committee approval obtained prior to participant enrolment.

intended_use.indication: GlucoTrack Lite is intended for use by adults with a confirmed diagnosis of diabetes (Type 1 or Type 2) as an adjunct self-management tool. The application logs user-entered blood glucose readings, displays historical trends, and delivers rule-based or algorithm-driven lifestyle nudges — including guidance on dietary choices, physical activity, and hydration — intended to support non-pharmacological self-management of blood glucose levels. It is not intended to replace clinical consultation, prescribe medication adjustments, or function as a primary diagnostic device.
intended_use.intended_user: The primary intended user is the adult diabetic patient operating the application independently in a non-clinical, self-care setting. Secondary users may include informal caregivers or family members who assist in data entry or review of trend summaries. The application is not intended for use by healthcare professionals as a primary clinical decision-support tool, nor is it intended for use in paediatric populations without appropriate clinical supervision.
intended_use.use_environment: GlucoTrack Lite is intended for use in non-clinical, home or community settings on commercially available Android or iOS smartphones meeting minimum hardware and operating system specifications defined in the device's technical documentation. The application operates via a patient-facing mobile interface and does not require connectivity to hospital IT infrastructure, electronic health records, or medical-grade monitoring equipment for its core functions.
intended_use.contraindications: The application is not intended for use as the sole basis for any insulin dosing decision or medication adjustment. It is not indicated for use in patients with Type 1 diabetes who require continuous clinical monitoring or closed-loop insulin delivery systems. The application is not intended for emergency use or for patients in hypoglycaemic or hyperglycaemic crisis. Users who experience sudden or severe changes in blood glucose should be directed to seek immediate clinical attention rather than rely on app-generated nudges.

device_description.components_architecture: GlucoTrack Lite is a software-only medical device comprising a patient-facing mobile application (Android and iOS), a cloud-based or on-device data storage and processing module, and a nudge-generation engine. The application architecture is expected to include: (1) a manual data-entry interface for glucose readings, meal logs, and activity records; (2) a trend visualisation module displaying time-series glucose data; (3) a rule-based or machine-learning nudge engine that generates personalised lifestyle recommendations based on logged inputs; and (4) a notification and alert delivery layer. Backend infrastructure, if any, is anticipated to comply with applicable data localisation requirements under the DPDP Act 2023.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The application operates by accepting user-entered blood glucose values — sourced from a separately CE- or CDSCO-approved glucometer — and contextual lifestyle inputs. The nudge engine applies predefined clinical rules and/or trained algorithmic models to identify patterns indicative of sub-optimal glucose management behaviour. Based on these patterns, the engine generates lifestyle nudges (e.g., dietary modifications, activity prompts, hydration reminders) delivered as in-app notifications. The application does not generate glucose measurements autonomously and does not interface directly with any hardware sensor in its current described form.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, GlucoTrack Lite has no physical material components requiring biocompatibility assessment. Applicable standards are expected to include IEC 62304:2006+AMD1:2015 (Medical device software — Software lifecycle processes), IEC 62366-1:2015 (Usability engineering), ISO 14971:2019 (Risk management), and ISO 27001 or equivalent for information security. DPDP Act 2023 compliance frameworks and data-at-rest encryption meeting IS/ISO/IEC 27002 guidance are expected to govern data handling. Compliance with applicable CDSCO SaMD guidance, including the October 2025 draft, is anticipated.
device_description.variants_accessories: In its current described form, GlucoTrack Lite is a single-variant application. No hardware accessories are bundled with the software. The application is compatible with readings from any commercially available, separately regulated blood glucose monitoring device. Future variants may include integration with continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) or wearable fitness trackers; such integrations would require a separate regulatory assessment and potential reclassification review.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: The software lifecycle is expected to be managed under a documented Software Development and Maintenance Plan aligned with IEC 62304 Class B software requirements. Version updates that materially alter the nudge algorithm's clinical outputs or intended use are expected to be evaluated under the Algorithm Change Protocol and, where applicable, notified to the licensing authority. Retirement of the application or specific software versions is expected to include user notification, secure deletion of any cloud-stored personal health data consistent with DPDP Act obligations, and closure procedures documented within the QMS.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD risk framework, significance of information is assessed by the degree to which the SaMD output is intended to drive, inform, or diagnose/treat. GlucoTrack Lite's lifestyle nudges are intended to influence user behaviour in managing a chronic condition — placing significance at 'drive clinical management' rather than 'treat or diagnose'. The healthcare situation is assessed as 'non-serious': Type 2 diabetes self-management nudges in a community setting, where the condition is chronic but not immediately life-threatening and where the SaMD output supplements rather than replaces clinical care. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' × 'non-serious condition' maps to IMDRF Category II. This assessment is conditional on nudge outputs remaining lifestyle-oriented; any shift toward insulin-dose guidance or acute clinical decision support would elevate the situation to 'serious', escalating the category to III.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, an IMDRF Category II SaMD is expected to correspond to CDSCO Class B. GlucoTrack Lite's intended function — providing lifestyle nudges based on user-logged glucose data for a non-serious chronic condition — is consistent with the Class B profile: moderate risk, non-life-sustaining, with outputs that supplement rather than replace clinical decision-making. The Class B designation is subject to change if the nudge algorithm is repositioned to provide condition-specific clinical recommendations (e.g., medication guidance), which may trigger a Class C assessment requiring central licensing authority review. Class B licensure is anticipated via state authority under MD-5, consistent with the product's current described risk and intended use profile.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Diabetes affects an estimated 101 million adults in India (ICMR-INDIAB 2023), with a substantial proportion managing the condition in community and home settings with limited access to continuous clinical supervision. Behavioural self-management — including dietary discipline, physical activity, and glucose monitoring adherence — is a clinically recognised pillar of Type 2 diabetes management per ADA and RSSDI guidelines. Digital tools that reinforce these behaviours between clinical visits address a documented care gap: patients frequently lack timely, personalised feedback on lifestyle decisions that influence glycaemic control. GlucoTrack Lite is positioned to address this gap by providing low-latency, contextually relevant nudges derived from the patient's own logged data, thereby supporting adherence to self-management behaviours in a scalable, cost-accessible format.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: International predicate devices operating in a comparable functional space include mySugr (Roche, CE-marked, FDA-registered), Dario Health (FDA 510(k) cleared for glucose logging and lifestyle management), and One Drop (FDA De Novo authorised for diabetes coaching). These products demonstrate an established regulatory acceptance pathway for glucose-logging and nudge-based SaMD at a moderate-risk classification level. Within India, no directly equivalent CDSCO-licensed SaMD predicate has been publicly identified in the current search scope, reflecting the nascent state of the domestic SaMD registry. The international predicate landscape is expected to support the Class B classification rationale and inform the evidence framework.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence plan for GlucoTrack Lite is expected to comprise three components: (1) a literature review substantiating the clinical effectiveness of lifestyle nudges in diabetes self-management, drawing on peer-reviewed studies of comparable digital interventions; (2) a usability study conducted per IEC 62366-1 with representative Indian diabetic users to validate the interface and nudge comprehension; and (3) a prospective pilot clinical study, registered on the Clinical Trials Registry – India (CTRI), measuring nudge engagement rates, glucose logging adherence, and HbA1c or fasting plasma glucose change over 90 days in a sample of adult Type 2 diabetic participants. Ethics Committee approval from a CDSCO-recognised committee is expected to be obtained prior to enrolment. This evidence plan is subject to revision following formal classification confirmation.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Pre-Specified Change Control Plan (PCCP) is recommended for GlucoTrack Lite given the presence of a rule-based or machine-learning nudge engine whose parameters may be updated as real-world performance data accumulates. The PCCP is expected to define the categories of algorithm change considered predetermined and within-scope (e.g., updating nudge trigger thresholds within pre-validated ranges, adding new lifestyle categories without altering clinical logic) versus changes that constitute a significant modification requiring fresh regulatory notification (e.g., introduction of a new AI model architecture, expansion of intended use to include medication guidance, or material changes to output interpretation logic). The PCCP document is expected to be maintained within the QMS and reviewed at each major software release cycle.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The algorithm change protocol is expected to specify: (1) a version-controlled change log for all nudge-engine modifications; (2) a risk assessment per ISO 14971 for each proposed change, categorising it as minor, moderate, or major; (3) performance regression testing requirements — including minimum dataset size and acceptance criteria — before any change is deployed to production; (4) internal sign-off requirements from the regulatory affairs function for moderate and major changes; and (5) a defined notification pathway to the licensing authority for changes assessed as significant modifications under MDR 2017 and applicable SaMD guidance. All algorithm changes are expected to be traceable to the original design specification and risk management file.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring for the nudge algorithm is expected to include: (1) continuous logging of nudge delivery rates, user engagement rates (open, acted-upon, dismissed), and opt-out patterns, aggregated at population level in a de-identified format; (2) periodic review (at minimum quarterly) of user-reported adverse experiences or complaints that may indicate nudge outputs are being misinterpreted as clinical instructions; (3) linkage, where user consent is obtained, to self-reported HbA1c or fasting glucose outcomes to assess longitudinal effectiveness signals; and (4) a defined performance drift detection threshold — if engagement or outcome metrics fall below pre-specified acceptable ranges, a formal algorithm review is triggered. Monitoring data is expected to feed into annual Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) reports maintained under the QMS.

executive_summary.body:
CardioAI Triage is an artificial intelligence software product intended to analyse 12-lead ECG images and flag findings suggestive of acute myocardial infarction (MI) for clinician review. Based on its intended use — supporting time-critical diagnostic decisions in a serious clinical state — the product is likely a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) within the scope of the Medical Device Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance. The product is expected to be classified as Class C under CDSCO MDR 2017, mapping to IMDRF SaMD Category III (drives clinical management of a serious situation).

The regulatory pathway is anticipated to involve an MD-12 Test Licence to enable clinical investigation in India, followed by an MD-9 Manufacturing Licence application to the Central Licensing Authority. Because the product incorporates a machine-learning model, an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) — including a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) and post-market real-world performance monitoring strategy — is expected to accompany the application, in line with the evolving CDSCO position on adaptive AI/ML SaMD.

The applicant's current readiness posture is early. No ISO 13485 Quality Management System, IEC 62304 software lifecycle process, or Ethics Committee-cleared clinical validation has yet been established. The model exists at a proof-of-principle stage, and no predicate device has been identified in the Indian market, indicating a novel-device pathway. Indicative time-to-approval is in the range of 18–30 months, contingent on parallel investment in QMS build-out, software-lifecycle documentation, and a prospective clinical validation study at a cardiology centre.

Other regulations likely to apply include the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (sensitive health data processing) and the ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023 (clinical validation oversight). ABDM and NABH alignment may become contractually relevant depending on hospital deployment channels. This Draft Pack outlines the intended use, device description, risk classification rationale, clinical context, and AI change-control posture as a starting basis for the formal CDSCO submission package.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic novel SaMD pathway — MD-12 Test Licence for clinical investigation followed by MD-9 Manufacturing Licence application to the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, with an Algorithm Change Protocol expected to be submitted alongside the application
headline_gaps: Establish an ISO 13485 Quality Management System aligned to MDR 2017 Schedule 5 — engage an experienced medical device QMS consultant for a gap assessment.
Implement an IEC 62304-compliant software lifecycle process and map the existing ML development pipeline to a defined software safety class with formal V&V records.
Initiate a prospective clinical validation study at a NABH-accredited or ICMR-networked cardiology centre, with an Ethics Committee-approved protocol, as the longest-lead item on the critical path.

intended_use.indication: CardioAI Triage is intended to assist qualified clinicians in the interpretation of 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) images by flagging findings suggestive of acute myocardial infarction. The output is intended as a clinical decision-support indicator and is not intended to be used as a sole basis for diagnosis or treatment. Final diagnostic and therapeutic decisions are expected to rest with the treating physician, taking into account clinical history, examination findings, biomarkers, and other investigations.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are licensed medical practitioners, including emergency physicians, cardiologists, internal medicine specialists, and trained physician assistants operating under physician supervision. The product is not intended for use by patients, lay caregivers, or untrained personnel. Users are expected to be familiar with ECG interpretation principles and to apply clinical judgement when reviewing the software's outputs.
intended_use.use_environment: The product is intended for use in healthcare settings including hospital emergency departments, cardiac care units, outpatient cardiology clinics, and diagnostic centres where 12-lead ECG acquisition is routinely performed. The software runs on standard hospital IT infrastructure or secure cloud deployment and is intended for use during clinical workflow at the point of ECG review. Deployment in home-use, ambulatory self-monitoring, or unsupervised consumer environments is outside the intended use.
intended_use.contraindications: The product is not intended for use on paediatric ECGs, on ECGs with non-standard lead configurations, on tracings with significant motion or electrical artefact that compromise image quality, or in patients with paced rhythms, pre-existing left bundle branch block, or other confounding conduction abnormalities where MI detection on surface ECG is known to be unreliable. The product is not intended to replace clinical assessment, troponin testing, or coronary imaging.

device_description.components_architecture: CardioAI Triage is a standalone Software as a Medical Device. The system comprises (i) an image-ingestion module that accepts digital ECG images in standard formats, (ii) a pre-processing pipeline that performs lead segmentation, signal extraction, and image normalisation, (iii) a deep-learning inference engine trained to identify ECG patterns associated with acute myocardial infarction, (iv) a clinician-facing output module that presents a flag, confidence indicator, and underlying lead-level highlights, and (v) an audit and logging subsystem. The product has no patient-contacting hardware components.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software receives a 12-lead ECG image, segments the individual leads, and applies a trained convolutional neural network to extract morphological features associated with ischaemic and infarction patterns (including ST-segment elevation, ST depression, T-wave inversion, and pathological Q waves). The model produces a probability score, which is thresholded to generate a binary 'MI suggested / not suggested' flag together with a confidence level. Flagged outputs are presented to the clinician for review alongside the original tracing, with the clinician retaining full diagnostic authority.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, the product does not involve patient-contacting materials and biocompatibility standards do not apply. Applicable standards expected to be referenced include IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle processes), ISO 14971 (risk management), ISO 13485 (quality management systems), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), and ISO/IEC 27001 alignment for information security. AI-specific guidance from the IMDRF SaMD Working Group and the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft is expected to inform the documentation set.
device_description.variants_accessories: A single product variant is anticipated at initial submission. The product has no physical accessories. Compatible ECG acquisition devices and image formats will be specified in the labelling. Future variants extending the indication (for example, to additional arrhythmias or other ischaemic syndromes) are anticipated to be handled through separate change-control submissions or through the Predetermined Change Control Plan, as appropriate.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As a software product, lifecycle management is governed by version control, change management, and post-market surveillance procedures rather than by physical disposal. End-of-life is expected to be managed through a controlled deprecation process, including customer notification, secure data handling, and removal of inference services in accordance with DPDP Act 2023 obligations on data deletion and retention.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: The product provides information that drives clinical management — a flag for possible acute myocardial infarction directly influences whether a clinician escalates the patient to urgent cardiology review, troponin testing, or catheterisation. Acute MI is a serious clinical condition in which delayed or missed diagnosis carries a high risk of morbidity and mortality, and false-positive flags may trigger unnecessary invasive workup. Mapping the device onto the IMDRF SaMD framework, 'drive clinical management × serious situation' falls within Category III. The product does not replace definitive diagnosis (which still rests on clinical assessment, troponin, and imaging), so it does not meet the threshold for Category IV (treat or diagnose × critical situation).
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017 Schedule 1 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, software intended to support diagnosis or drive clinical management in a serious clinical state is typically placed in Class C (moderate-to-high risk). This aligns with the IMDRF Category III determination above. Class C designation places the product under the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority and likely requires an MD-12 Test Licence ahead of clinical investigation and an MD-9 Manufacturing Licence for commercial release. The novel nature of the device — with no identified Indian predicate — reinforces the higher classification call and the need for a complete clinical evidence dossier and Algorithm Change Protocol. The applicant intends to confirm the classification through a CDSCO pre-submission engagement.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Acute myocardial infarction remains a leading cause of mortality in India, and time-to-diagnosis is a critical determinant of outcome. ECG interpretation at the point of first medical contact is variable, particularly in non-specialist settings such as emergency triage, peripheral hospitals, and outpatient clinics where on-site cardiology expertise may not be available. A reliable AI-based decision-support flag has the potential to reduce time-to-recognition of ischaemic ECG patterns and to support earlier escalation. The product is positioned as an adjunct to, not a replacement for, clinician interpretation and downstream diagnostic workup.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No directly equivalent predicate device approved by CDSCO has been identified in the Indian market for AI-based MI flagging on ECG images. International precedents exist for AI-enabled ECG analysis software cleared by other regulators, which may inform the technical and clinical evidence approach but do not constitute Indian predicates. The applicant therefore intends to pursue the novel-device pathway and to substantiate safety and performance through a prospective clinical validation study conducted in India.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The proposed clinical evidence package is expected to comprise (i) analytical performance testing on a curated retrospective dataset of Indian-population ECGs with adjudicated ground truth, reporting sensitivity, specificity, and area-under-curve metrics with confidence intervals; (ii) a prospective clinical validation study at one or more NABH-accredited or ICMR-networked cardiology centres under an Ethics Committee-approved protocol, comparing model output against expert cardiologist adjudication and clinical outcomes; and (iii) a usability evaluation under IEC 62366-1. The evidence plan will be developed in alignment with ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023 and the CDSCO clinical investigation framework under MDR 2017 Chapter VII.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is anticipated to define the scope of model updates that may be implemented post-approval without a fresh regulatory submission. The PCCP is expected to specify the types of changes covered (for example, retraining on additional Indian-population data, threshold recalibration, performance maintenance updates), the bounded performance envelope within which such changes may occur, and the changes that fall outside the PCCP and would trigger a separate CDSCO notification or fresh approval (for example, indication expansion, change in intended user, or addition of new clinical claims).
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol is expected to describe the controlled procedures by which model updates within the PCCP envelope are developed, validated, documented, and released. Anticipated elements include data governance and dataset version control, model retraining and revalidation procedures, pre-release performance acceptance criteria benchmarked against the original approved performance, regression testing against held-out reference datasets, configuration management aligned to IEC 62304, and traceable change records maintained within the ISO 13485 quality system. Each in-scope update is expected to be logged and made available to CDSCO on request.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: A post-market real-world performance monitoring programme is anticipated, comprising continuous logging of model outputs, periodic sampling of cases for clinician adjudication, monitoring for performance drift across demographic and clinical subgroups, tracking of false-positive and false-negative rates against site-level outcome data where available, and a defined trigger framework for corrective action — including model rollback, threshold adjustment, or field safety notice — should performance fall outside acceptance bounds. Findings are expected to be summarised in periodic post-market surveillance reports submitted in line with MDR 2017 obligations and the CDSCO SaMD draft expectations on lifecycle oversight of AI/ML devices.

executive_summary.body:
CardioAI Triage is an artificial-intelligence-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) that analyses ECG images and generates flags indicating possible myocardial infarction (MI). The product is intended to assist clinicians in time-critical triage decisions and is expected to be classified as a Class C SaMD under the Medical Device Rules 2017 (MDR 2017) and the CDSCO Draft Guidance on SaMD (October 2025). This classification reflects the IMDRF Category III designation — software whose output is intended to drive clinical management in a serious healthcare situation — carrying a high inherent risk profile independent of the product's current development stage.

The regulatory pathway for CardioAI Triage is anticipated to involve a Central Licensing Authority application under the evolving SaMD-specific CDSCO framework. Based on current published guidance, the submission is expected to require an MD-9 application for market authorisation supported by a Clinical Evaluation Report, a full Technical Documentation File aligned with MDR 2017 Schedule 5, an IEC 62304-compliant Software Development File, and an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) addressing the product's AI/ML adaptive components. A prior MD-12 Test License may be sought to enable the formal clinical investigation required to generate Indian patient-level evidence.

The product is currently assessed at a red readiness posture (readiness score 2 of 10) and Technology Readiness Level 2. No quality management system, IEC 62304-mapped software lifecycle process, or clinical validation programme has been detected. Three material gaps are immediately on the critical path: establishing an ISO 13485-compliant QMS, commissioning an IEC 62304 software safety class determination for the ML pipeline, and initiating Ethics Committee engagement for a prospective clinical validation study at an ICMR-networked or NABH-accredited cardiology centre. Addressing these foundational elements in parallel is expected to compress the estimated 18–30 month pathway to CDSCO market authorisation.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing / software development — MD-9 application to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority for Class C SaMD market authorisation, preceded by MD-12 Test License application to enable Ethics Committee-approved clinical investigation; Algorithm Change Protocol submission anticipated alongside the MD-9 dossier under the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance

intended_use.indication: CardioAI Triage is intended to analyse resting 12-lead ECG images and generate a binary or probabilistic flag indicating the possible presence of findings consistent with acute myocardial infarction. The output is intended to assist qualified healthcare professionals in prioritising patient triage in emergency, cardiac care, or outpatient settings. The device is not intended to replace physician diagnosis or to be the sole basis for clinical decision-making; it is intended to function as a decision-support adjunct within a supervised clinical workflow.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended primary users are qualified medical professionals, including emergency medicine physicians, cardiologists, and trained clinical staff operating under physician supervision, who have competency in ECG interpretation and clinical management of acute coronary syndromes. The device is not intended for use by lay persons or for direct-to-consumer self-diagnosis. Operator training on device outputs and their limitations is expected to be a prerequisite for clinical deployment.
intended_use.use_environment: CardioAI Triage is intended for use in regulated healthcare environments including hospital emergency departments, cardiac care units, outpatient cardiology clinics, and telemedicine triage platforms operating under institutional governance. The software is expected to operate on validated hardware and network infrastructure meeting applicable data security requirements under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023. Deployment in resource-limited or unmonitored settings without qualified clinical oversight is outside the intended use.
intended_use.contraindications: CardioAI Triage is not intended for use as a standalone diagnostic tool in the absence of qualified clinical oversight. The device is not validated for paediatric ECG interpretation, pacemaker-dependent rhythms, or ECG images of insufficient technical quality as defined in the product's technical specifications. The device output should not be used to initiate irreversible clinical interventions — such as thrombolytic therapy — without confirmatory physician assessment. Use in settings where real-time clinical review of AI flags is not operationally feasible is considered outside the intended use boundary.

device_description.components_architecture: CardioAI Triage is a cloud-hosted or on-premise software system comprising three principal architectural layers: (1) an image ingestion and pre-processing module that accepts digitised 12-lead ECG images in standard formats (e.g., JPEG, PNG, PDF, or HL7-compliant DICOM where applicable), normalises image resolution, and extracts lead-level waveform representations; (2) a trained deep learning inference engine — likely based on a convolutional or transformer-based neural network architecture — that processes the extracted waveform features and generates a probability score and binary flag for possible MI-consistent findings; and (3) a results delivery layer that surfaces the flag, confidence indicator, and relevant waveform highlights to the clinical user interface. An administrative module for audit logging, user access control, and model version management is also expected to form part of the deployed system.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device applies supervised machine learning, likely using a convolutional neural network or equivalent deep learning architecture, trained on labelled ECG image datasets containing confirmed MI and non-MI cases. During inference, the model extracts morphological and temporal features from the uploaded ECG image — including ST-segment elevation or depression, Q-wave characteristics, T-wave inversions, and bundle branch block patterns — and maps these features to a learned decision boundary. The output is a probabilistic score that is thresholded to produce a binary triage flag. The model's performance characteristics — sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value — are expected to be prospectively validated on an independent Indian patient cohort prior to CDSCO submission.
device_description.materials_standards: As a pure software device, CardioAI Triage does not incorporate physical materials requiring biocompatibility assessment. Applicable standards are expected to include IEC 62304 (Medical device software — software life cycle processes), IEC 82304-1 (Health software — general requirements for safety), ISO 14971 (Application of risk management to medical devices), ISO 13485 (Quality management systems), and ISO/IEC 27001 (Information security management) for data handling. Alignment with IEC 62366-1 (Usability engineering) is anticipated for the clinical user interface. Reference to IMDRF guidance on SaMD and the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft is expected throughout the technical file.
device_description.variants_accessories: At the time of this submission draft, CardioAI Triage is described as a single-configuration software product. If deployment variants exist — for example, a cloud-hosted SaaS version and an on-premise hospital-integrated version — each variant with a distinct software architecture or intended use boundary is expected to be documented separately within the technical file and may require individual consideration within the CDSCO application. No physical accessories are associated with the software device itself; the ECG acquisition hardware used to generate input images is outside the device boundary and is assumed to be separately regulated.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: The software lifecycle is expected to be managed in accordance with IEC 62304, covering planned and unplanned maintenance, post-market surveillance-triggered updates, and algorithm version changes governed by the Algorithm Change Protocol. End-of-life for a software version is expected to involve formal retirement documentation, customer communication, and data retention compliance under applicable Indian health data regulations. As a software product, physical disposal obligations do not apply; data deletion and de-commissioning procedures for hosted instances are expected to be defined within the product's post-market and data governance documentation.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorisation Framework (N12 FINAL:2014), risk category is determined by the intersection of the significance of information provided by the SaMD and the healthcare situation or condition. CardioAI Triage provides information that is intended to drive clinical management — the triage flag is intended to prompt immediate escalation or prioritisation of the patient, directly influencing care pathway decisions rather than merely informing or alerting. The healthcare situation is serious: acute myocardial infarction is a life-threatening condition characterised by irreversible myocardial injury where delayed or incorrect triage directly increases morbidity and mortality risk. The combination of 'drive clinical management' × 'serious condition' maps to IMDRF Category III. A false negative could delay time-critical reperfusion therapy; a false positive could misallocate emergency resources. Both failure modes carry clinically significant consequences.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Device Rules 2017 and the CDSCO October 2025 Draft Guidance on SaMD, software devices are classified using a risk-based framework broadly aligned with the IMDRF categorisation. IMDRF Category III SaMD — software providing information to drive clinical management in a serious condition — is expected to correspond to Class C under the CDSCO framework, representing high-risk devices whose failure or misuse could cause serious injury or contribute to patient harm. Class C is analogous to EU MDR Class IIb and is subject to Central Licensing Authority review, mandatory clinical evaluation with Indian patient data, and post-market surveillance obligations. The AI/ML character of the device and the ACP requirement further support a Class C designation under the evolving SaMD-specific regulatory provisions.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Acute myocardial infarction remains one of the leading causes of mortality and morbidity in India, with the Indian subcontinent bearing a disproportionately high burden of premature cardiovascular disease. Time-to-treatment — particularly time to percutaneous coronary intervention or thrombolysis — is the dominant determinant of outcomes in ST-elevation MI. A significant proportion of Indian patients present to primary care centres, district hospitals, or emergency departments where 24×7 expert cardiologist coverage for ECG interpretation is not available, creating a clinically and operationally meaningful gap in early, accurate MI triage. An AI-assisted ECG flagging tool that can provide reliable, rapid, and consistent identification of possible MI-consistent patterns — as a support layer for the treating clinician — addresses a documented unmet need in the Indian healthcare delivery context, particularly in Tier 2 and Tier 3 settings.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No directly equivalent AI-based ECG MI flagging SaMD is known to have received CDSCO market authorisation at the time of this draft, which positions CardioAI Triage on the novel device pathway rather than a predicate-reliance pathway. Internationally, devices such as the FDA-authorised ECG analysis algorithms from GE Healthcare, Philips, and AliveCor, as well as CE-marked equivalents, provide partial functional comparators for the purposes of technical benchmarking and literature-based clinical evidence support. The technical documentation is expected to reference the performance benchmarks, validation datasets, and intended use boundaries of these international comparators, while acknowledging that Indian-specific clinical validation on a representative ECG dataset is expected by CDSCO for a Class C SaMD authorisation.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy for CardioAI Triage is anticipated to comprise two components. First, a systematic literature review and meta-analysis of published AI ECG MI detection performance studies, including studies using datasets representing South Asian patient populations where available, is expected to establish a clinical evidence foundation for the technical file. Second, and more critically, a prospective single-arm or comparative clinical investigation on Indian patients, conducted at one or more NABH-accredited or ICMR-networked cardiology centres under an Ethics Committee-approved protocol, is expected to be required to generate locally applicable sensitivity, specificity, and clinical impact data. The clinical investigation protocol is expected to define the reference standard (e.g., adjudicated clinical diagnosis of STEMI or NSTEMI with troponin and imaging correlation), the target patient population, the performance acceptance criteria, and the statistical plan. This study is the longest-lead element of the regulatory programme and is recommended for initiation at the earliest feasible stage.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Pre-Determined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to be submitted alongside the MD-9 dossier for CardioAI Triage, in accordance with the anticipated requirements of the CDSCO October 2025 SaMD draft guidance and aligned with IMDRF guidance on 'Machine Learning-enabled Medical Devices' (2021). The PCCP is expected to specify the categories of algorithm modifications that may be implemented post-authorisation without requiring a full re-submission — likely including re-training on expanded datasets within the validated patient population and demographic envelope, threshold recalibration within pre-specified performance bounds, and minor software updates that do not alter the algorithm architecture or intended use. Changes outside these pre-specified categories — such as expansion to new ECG pattern classes, new intended populations, or architectural changes to the inference model — are expected to require a change notification or new application to CDSCO.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol is expected to define a structured governance process for all post-market modifications to the CardioAI Triage inference model. The protocol is anticipated to include: (1) a change classification procedure that assigns each proposed modification to a PCCP-covered or non-covered category; (2) a performance evaluation requirement specifying the minimum validation dataset size, statistical performance thresholds, and independent test set requirements that must be met before any model version is deployed; (3) a version control and audit trail procedure integrated with the IEC 62304-compliant software configuration management system; and (4) a regulatory notification decision tree that maps each change category to the applicable CDSCO reporting obligation. The protocol is expected to be a living document reviewed at defined intervals as part of the post-market surveillance system.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring for CardioAI Triage is expected to form a core component of the post-market surveillance plan required under MDR 2017. The monitoring programme is anticipated to include: systematic collection of clinician feedback and override events (instances where the AI flag was overridden by the treating physician and the clinical outcome), periodic retrospective audit of flagged versus confirmed MI cases at participating clinical sites to assess real-world sensitivity and specificity drift, automated monitoring of input data distribution for signs of data drift relative to the training and validation dataset characteristics, and adverse event and near-miss reporting through the CDSCO vigilance system. Findings from real-world monitoring are expected to feed back into the PCCP review cycle and inform decisions on whether algorithm recalibration or re-training is warranted.

executive_summary.body:
SurgiPlan XR is an extended-reality (XR) surgical planning software intended to assist orthopedic surgeons in pre-operative implant placement planning. Based on the IMDRF SaMD framework and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, the device likely falls within IMDRF Category III — driving clinical management in a serious healthcare situation — and is expected to be classified as a Class C medical device under the Medical Device Rules 2017. The product incorporates AI/ML-based decision support, which triggers Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) considerations and elevates the technical documentation burden under IEC 62304 and ISO 14971.

No predicate device has been identified on the Indian market, and the manufacturer is presumed to be domestic. The expected pathway is a domestic manufacturing application to the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, beginning with an MD-12 test license to enable clinical investigation, followed by an MD-9 manufacturing license post clinical validation. Parallel ICMR Ethics Committee engagement and CTRI registration are likely required given the AI/ML clinical decision support nature of the device.

Current readiness is assessed at the earliest stage. No Quality Management System, Design History File, risk management file, software lifecycle documentation, or clinical evidence has been detected. The indicative regulatory timeline is 24–42 months, anchored to QMS establishment, technical documentation build, MD-12 test license, clinical investigation execution, and final MD-9 review. Compliance with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 is also expected from day one of any Indian deployment given the processing of patient imaging and clinical records. NABH Digital Health Standards alignment is likely to be a de-facto gate for hospital procurement. The most material near-term priority is parallel initiation of QMS scaffolding, technical documentation, and clinical site engagement, since these items sit on the critical path and cannot be compressed late in the program.

intended_use.indication: SurgiPlan XR is intended to assist qualified orthopedic surgeons in pre-operative planning of implant placement by providing extended-reality visualisation of patient-specific anatomical models derived from diagnostic imaging, together with AI-based decision support for implant sizing, positioning, and trajectory selection. The output is intended to inform, not replace, surgeon judgement during surgical planning for orthopedic procedures involving implantable devices.
intended_use.intended_user: Qualified orthopedic surgeons and surgical planning staff working under the supervision of a registered medical practitioner. Use is expected to be restricted to trained users who have completed a manufacturer-provided training programme on XR navigation, visualisation conventions, and the limitations of AI-based decision support outputs.
intended_use.use_environment: The device is intended for use in pre-operative planning environments within tertiary and secondary hospital settings, including orthopedic departments, surgical planning suites, and dedicated XR planning workstations. The product is not intended for intra-operative real-time guidance in the current scope, and is not intended for unsupervised use by patients or non-clinical users.
intended_use.contraindications: The device is not intended for use as the sole basis for surgical decision-making, for intra-operative navigation in the current scope, or in clinical scenarios where the underlying diagnostic imaging is of insufficient quality to generate a reliable anatomical model. Use in paediatric anatomy, pathological bone conditions, or anatomical variants outside the validated population is likely outside the intended use until specific clinical evidence is generated. Final contraindications are expected to be refined based on clinical investigation outcomes.

device_description.components_architecture: SurgiPlan XR is a Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) comprising a DICOM image ingestion module, a segmentation and 3D reconstruction engine, an AI/ML-based implant recommendation module, an XR visualisation layer rendered on compatible head-mounted displays or workstation monitors, and a planning report generator. The architecture is expected to follow a modular client-server or hybrid cloud design, with patient data processed in compliance with the DPDP Act 2023. No physical hardware is manufactured by the applicant; supported XR display devices are commercially available off-the-shelf items integrated as accessories.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device ingests patient-specific diagnostic imaging (typically CT or MRI in DICOM format), performs automated anatomical segmentation, and generates a 3D model of the surgical site. An AI/ML model trained on annotated orthopedic case data proposes candidate implant sizes, orientations, and placement trajectories. The surgeon reviews and may modify proposals within an XR visualisation environment, and the finalised plan is exported as a structured pre-operative report. The system is intended as decision support; the surgeon retains full clinical authority over the final plan.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, no patient-contacting materials apply. The product is expected to be developed and maintained in conformance with ISO 13485:2016 (QMS), IEC 62304:2006+AMD1:2015 (medical device software lifecycle), ISO 14971:2019 (risk management), IEC 62366-1:2015 (usability engineering), and IEC 82304-1:2016 (health software). Cybersecurity controls are likely to be aligned with IMDRF cybersecurity guidance and emerging CDSCO expectations. AI/ML lifecycle considerations are expected to follow ICMR's 2023 Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare.
device_description.variants_accessories: Supported accessories typically include commercially available XR head-mounted displays and standard medical-grade workstation monitors. No proprietary hardware variants are anticipated in the initial submission. Software variants may exist for different orthopedic anatomical regions (for example, hip, knee, spine), and each variant is expected to be scoped within its own intended use statement and clinical evidence package.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As software, end-of-life is managed through controlled de-commissioning: revocation of user access, secure erasure of locally cached patient data, and archival of regulatory records per CDSCO retention expectations. Software updates and version control are expected to be governed under the QMS change control procedure, with a defined Algorithm Change Protocol for AI/ML model updates. No physical disposal considerations apply to the software itself.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: SurgiPlan XR provides AI-based recommendations on implant sizing, positioning, and trajectory that directly inform a surgeon's pre-operative orthopedic plan. Under the IMDRF SaMD framework (N12), software that informs implant selection and placement is typically considered to drive clinical management, since the output materially shapes the intended surgical intervention. Orthopedic implant procedures are typically performed in serious healthcare situations where incorrect implant placement may lead to revision surgery, functional impairment, or chronic pain. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' significance with a 'serious' healthcare situation maps to IMDRF Category III. This positioning is consistent with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft, which reinforces regulatory oversight of software influencing surgical clinical management.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Device Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft, software intended to drive clinical management decisions in serious clinical states is likely to be classified as Class C. The IMDRF Category III positioning supports this classification, and the AI/ML-CDS qualifier further reinforces the moderate-high risk profile. Class C entails central authority oversight via CDSCO HQ, with manufacturing licensing under MD-9 and a likely prerequisite clinical investigation under an MD-12 test license. No predicate device has been identified on the Indian market, which removes the option of predicate-based equivalence and is expected to require a full clinical investigation pathway. Final classification is subject to CDSCO determination, ideally confirmed via a pre-submission meeting.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Orthopedic implant procedures, including arthroplasty and trauma fixation, are sensitive to implant sizing and placement accuracy. Mal-positioning is a known contributor to revision surgery, residual pain, and functional limitation. In the Indian context, surgical volumes are growing while access to advanced pre-operative planning tools remains uneven across tier-2 and tier-3 hospitals. SurgiPlan XR is intended to address this by providing patient-specific 3D planning and AI-supported implant recommendations within an XR environment, with the goal of improving planning consistency and reducing intra-operative uncertainty.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No clear Indian predicate device has been identified for an XR-based AI-CDS orthopedic surgical planning tool at the time of this draft. Internationally, several orthopedic planning platforms exist with varying levels of AI integration and XR visualisation, but their Indian regulatory status is uncertain. Given the absence of an identified Indian predicate, the submission is likely to proceed via a full clinical investigation pathway rather than a predicate-equivalence route. A formal predicate search via CDSCO's medical device database and a pre-submission meeting are recommended to confirm this position.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy is expected to include a phased approach: bench validation of segmentation accuracy against ground-truth anatomical measurements; usability and human factors evaluation under IEC 62366-1 with representative orthopedic surgeons; and a prospective clinical investigation conducted under an MD-12 test license, with Institutional Ethics Committee approval and CTRI registration, evaluating planning accuracy and clinically meaningful endpoints against current standard-of-care planning. AI/ML performance evaluation is expected to follow ICMR's 2023 Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare, including bias assessment across relevant demographic and anatomical sub-groups. The investigation design should ideally be socialised with CDSCO via a pre-submission meeting before protocol finalisation.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to scope the categories of AI/ML model changes anticipated over the product lifecycle, including periodic retraining on expanded orthopedic case data, adjustments to segmentation models, and refinements to implant recommendation logic. The PCCP is likely to define the boundaries of changes that may be implemented under the protocol without a fresh CDSCO submission, versus changes that would trigger a new licensing event. The framework should be aligned with emerging CDSCO SaMD guidance and IMDRF good machine learning practice principles.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol is expected to specify, for each anticipated change category, the data management practices, retraining methodology, validation test plan, performance acceptance criteria, and rollback procedures. Documentation is expected to be maintained under the QMS change control procedure with traceability to the Design History File and risk management file. Sub-group performance, including bias evaluation across demographic and anatomical sub-populations, is likely to be a standing component of the validation test plan.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring is expected to include structured collection of planning outcomes, surgeon override rates, and any reported adverse events linked to planning recommendations. A periodic performance review cadence — typically quarterly in the early post-launch period — is recommended, with predefined thresholds that trigger investigation, model rollback, or a regulatory notification to CDSCO. Integration with the manufacturer's MDR vigilance and complaint-handling processes under the Medical Device Rules 2017 is expected to be formalised within the QMS.

executive_summary.body:
SurgiPlan XR is an extended-reality (XR) surgical planning software intended to support orthopedic surgeons in implant selection, sizing, and placement planning. Based on published guidance and the CDSCO Medical Device Rules 2017 (MDR 2017), the product is likely classified as a Class C Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) under the central authority pathway, given that it drives clinical management decisions in a serious healthcare situation. The applicable IMDRF category is III, reflecting the intersection of 'drive clinical management' significance and a 'serious' clinical condition.

The regulatory pathway for SurgiPlan XR is expected to proceed through the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, beginning with a Test License application (MD-12) to authorize a clinical investigation, followed by a Manufacturing/Import License application (MD-9) once clinical evidence is established. Given the absence of a clearly identified Indian predicate device, the investigational track is the expected route, and the overall approval timeline is estimated at 24–42 months from the commencement of quality system establishment.

No quality management system, design history file, risk management documentation, or clinical investigation evidence was detected during the readiness assessment. The product is currently assessed at TRL 2 (proof of principle), and readiness is rated in the lowest band, indicating near-total regulatory unpreparedness relative to the demands of a Class C SaMD submission. The three most material gaps—QMS absence, missing technical documentation, and no clinical validation activity—each represent critical path items that must be addressed in parallel and without delay.

The AI/ML adaptive component of SurgiPlan XR further requires an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) and a pre-defined Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP), in line with evolving CDSCO SaMD draft guidance published post-October 2025. Additionally, Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 obligations and ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023 are expected to apply from the commencement of any clinical investigation involving Indian patient data.

intended_use.use_environment: SurgiPlan XR is intended for use in regulated clinical environments including tertiary care hospitals, orthopedic specialty centers, and accredited surgical facilities. intended_use.contraindications: SurgiPlan XR is not intended to replace the independent clinical judgment of the treating orthopedic surgeon. The software is not intended for use in emergency intraoperative settings where preoperative planning has not been completed. device_description.components_architecture: SurgiPlan XR is a software-only medical device comprising a core planning engine, an XR visualization module, an imaging data ingestion layer (supporting DICOM-format inputs), an implant library database, and a user interface layer optimized for XR headset and workstation interaction. device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, SurgiPlan XR does not incorporate physical materials. device_description.variants_accessories: SurgiPlan XR may be offered in configuration variants corresponding to specific orthopedic subspecialty workflows (e.g., total knee arthroplasty, total hip arthroplasty, spine). device_description.lifecycle_disposal: As a software-only device, SurgiPlan XR does not generate physical waste requiring regulated disposal.
