Patient data processed by the software is subject to Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 retention and deletion obligations. Post-market surveillance obligations under MDR 2017 are expected to apply throughout the commercial lifecycle, including periodic safety update reporting and vigilance event reporting as applicable.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework, the significance of information provided by SurgiPlan XR is assessed as 'drive clinical management': the software generates a surgical plan specifying implant type, size, and placement parameters that directly informs and structures the operative intervention, rather than merely informing or treating in a secondary capacity. The healthcare situation is assessed as 'serious': orthopedic implant procedures address conditions (e.g., end-stage arthritis, fracture, deformity) that significantly impair quality of life and functional capacity, and an incorrectly planned implant placement may result in surgical complications, revision surgery, or long-term patient harm. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' × 'serious' maps to IMDRF SaMD Category III.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017 and the CDSCO SaMD classification framework (aligned with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance), Class C corresponds to SaMD that drives clinical management in a serious clinical situation — directly mirroring IMDRF Category III. SurgiPlan XR's function of generating actionable surgical plans for implant placement, in the context of orthopedic procedures carrying meaningful clinical risk, supports a Class C classification. The AI/ML component further elevates the classification consideration, as adaptive or learning algorithms influencing clinical outputs are treated with heightened scrutiny under the evolving CDSCO SaMD guidance. Class C requires central authority review, clinical investigation data, and a comprehensive technical dossier including software lifecycle documentation.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Accurate preoperative planning is a recognized determinant of orthopedic implant surgery outcomes. Mismatch in implant size or suboptimal placement is associated with increased rates of revision surgery, post-operative instability, and accelerated implant wear. Traditional templating methods using two-dimensional radiographs are subject to magnification error and limited three-dimensional anatomical insight. XR-based planning tools address this gap by enabling surgeons to interact with patient-specific three-dimensional anatomy in an immersive environment, potentially reducing planning errors and improving surgical confidence. In the Indian context, rising volumes of joint replacement and spinal implant procedures in both the public and private sectors reinforce the clinical relevance of improved planning accuracy.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No directly equivalent XR-based surgical planning SaMD has been identified as a cleared predicate on the CDSCO device register at the time of this draft. Internationally, comparable tools exist (e.g., Stryker MAKO SmartRobotics planning software, Zimmer Biomet ZBEdge, Materialise SurgiCase); however, these have not been confirmed as CDSCO-cleared predicates. The absence of a recognized Indian predicate is a material regulatory consideration: it places SurgiPlan XR on the full investigational track and is likely to require a clinical investigation generating India-specific safety and performance evidence rather than permitting reliance on substantial equivalence.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence plan for SurgiPlan XR is expected to include a prospective, controlled clinical investigation conducted at one or more NABH-accredited tertiary orthopedic centers in India. The primary endpoints are likely to include planning accuracy (measured against intraoperative findings), implant size prediction accuracy, and surgeon-assessed usability. Secondary endpoints may include operative time, surgical plan modification rate, and adverse event rates. The investigation protocol is expected to be reviewed and approved by an accredited Institutional Ethics Committee prior to commencement, with mandatory CTRI registration. AI/ML algorithm performance, including bias assessment across relevant patient demographic subgroups, is expected to be included in the clinical validation scope in line with ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: SurgiPlan XR is expected to require a Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) covering foreseeable modifications to the AI/ML algorithms responsible for anatomical segmentation, implant sizing recommendations, and placement optimization. The PCCP should define the scope of algorithm changes that may be implemented without requiring a full new regulatory submission (e.g., incremental model retraining within validated performance bounds), the conditions under which a new submission or substantial modification notification to CDSCO is triggered, and the performance boundary specifications below which a change is not permissible without regulatory review. The PCCP is expected to be included in the initial MD-9/MD-7 technical dossier in line with evolving CDSCO SaMD draft guidance.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The algorithm change protocol for SurgiPlan XR is expected to specify: (a) the types of planned algorithm modifications (e.g., model retraining on expanded datasets, addition of new implant system libraries, update of segmentation pipelines); (b) the pre-specified performance benchmarks and acceptance criteria that must be met or exceeded before any modified algorithm version is deployed; (c) the internal validation and verification process applicable to each change type; and (d) the regulatory notification and approval pathway for changes that exceed the PCCP-defined boundaries. Version control and change documentation are expected to be maintained in the software's IEC 62304-compliant change management system.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring for SurgiPlan XR's AI/ML components is expected to include: systematic collection of post-operative outcome data from clinical sites using the device, comparison of planned versus actual implant placement parameters, monitoring for distributional shift in input imaging data relative to training data characteristics, periodic model performance re-evaluation at defined intervals (suggested: semi-annually), and a vigilance mechanism for detecting and reporting unanticipated algorithm output anomalies. Monitoring findings are expected to feed into the post-market surveillance report and the periodic safety update process required under MDR 2017. Any detected performance degradation outside pre-specified thresholds should trigger the algorithm change protocol review process.

executive_summary.body:
StrokeFast is an AI-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) intended to analyze non-contrast CT brain scans and flag findings consistent with possible acute stroke, supporting radiologists and emergency physicians in triage and clinical management decisions. Based on the IMDRF SaMD framework, the device is likely positioned at the 'drive clinical management' significance tier within a 'critical' healthcare situation, mapping to IMDRF Category III. Under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, this likely corresponds to Class C, with central licensing authority oversight. Because StrokeFast incorporates an AI/ML model whose performance is expected to be retrained or refined over time, an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is anticipated to be part of the submission package.

The intended pathway is a domestic manufacturing route through CDSCO's central licensing authority, beginning with an MD-12 test license to enable clinical investigation in India, followed by an MD-9 manufacturing licence application supported by clinical performance evidence, ISO 13485-compliant QMS documentation, IEC 62304 software lifecycle records, IEC 62366 usability files, and an ACP aligned with the SaMD draft. Ethics Committee approval and CTRI registration are expected to precede any prospective clinical study.

Current readiness is assessed as low (1/10, red band): no quality system, no documented clinical validation on Indian populations, and no prior CDSCO engagement have been identified. A realistic timeline to approval is 24–36 months, contingent on QMS build-out, clinical study execution, and CDSCO review cycles. The sponsor is expected to prioritise QMS establishment, Ethics Committee engagement at a lead neuroimaging site, and a CDSCO pre-submission meeting via the SUGAM portal in parallel over the next 90 days. This Draft Pack provides submission-ready content that the sponsor may adapt for the corresponding CDSCO forms and supporting dossiers.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — MD-12 test license for clinical investigation, followed by MD-9 manufacturing licence to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, with Algorithm Change Protocol per Oct 2025 SaMD draft
headline_gaps: Establish ISO 13485 and IEC 62304 compliant QMS and software lifecycle documentation before site engagement.
Obtain Ethics Committee approval and CTRI registration for a prospective clinical performance study at a lead neuroimaging site.
Initiate CDSCO engagement via SUGAM, including a pre-submission meeting to confirm SaMD class and ACP expectations.

intended_use.indication: StrokeFast is intended as a computer-assisted triage and notification software that analyzes non-contrast CT brain images of adult patients presenting with suspected acute stroke and highlights studies likely to contain findings consistent with stroke (such as large vessel occlusion or established infarct patterns). The output is intended to support, and not replace, the interpreting clinician's diagnostic decision and is expected to be used alongside the original CT images and complete clinical context.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are typically qualified radiologists, neurologists, and emergency physicians working within hospital-based stroke care pathways. Use is expected to be limited to clinicians trained on the device's outputs, limitations, and recommended workflow integration. The device is not intended for use by patients or untrained personnel.
intended_use.use_environment: The device is intended for deployment in hospital radiology and emergency department environments where CT imaging is routinely performed for suspected stroke. Deployment configurations may include on-premise PACS-integrated installations or secure cloud-hosted instances with appropriate data protection controls aligned to DPDP Act 2023 obligations. Use is expected to occur within stroke-ready clinical workflows where a qualified clinician retains final diagnostic authority.
intended_use.contraindications: StrokeFast is not intended for use as a sole or primary diagnostic basis, for paediatric populations, for haemorrhagic stroke characterisation beyond the validated indication, or for imaging modalities other than non-contrast CT brain. The device is not intended for use where image quality is inadequate, where acquisition parameters fall outside the validated range, or where the clinical scenario falls outside the labelled indication.

device_description.components_architecture: StrokeFast is a standalone software medical device comprising: (i) a DICOM ingestion and pre-processing module that receives non-contrast CT brain studies from hospital PACS or modality worklists; (ii) an AI/ML inference engine based on a trained deep learning model that analyzes image volumes for findings consistent with possible stroke; (iii) a results module that generates a structured notification or worklist prioritisation output; and (iv) an audit, logging, and monitoring layer that records inputs, outputs, and model version metadata. The architecture is expected to support both on-premise and secure cloud deployments, with role-based access control and encrypted data transit and storage.
device_description.principle_of_operation: Once a non-contrast CT brain study is received, the software applies pre-processing steps (such as orientation normalisation, skull stripping, and intensity windowing) and passes the volumetric image data through a trained neural network. The model produces a probability or categorical indicator of findings consistent with possible stroke. Outputs are routed back to the clinician's workflow as a notification, worklist flag, or structured report element. The software is intended as an adjunct, with the interpreting clinician retaining full diagnostic responsibility based on the original images and clinical context.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, StrokeFast does not involve patient-contacting materials. Development is expected to be aligned with IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle), ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), IEC 82304-1 (health software safety), and applicable parts of IEC 81001-5-1 (cybersecurity). Data handling is expected to align with DPDP Act 2023 obligations and ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare (2023).
device_description.variants_accessories: Initial submission is anticipated to cover a single software variant, with deployment configuration options (on-premise vs. cloud) documented as part of the technical file. No physical accessories are bundled. Integration interfaces (DICOM, HL7/FHIR) are documented as part of the interoperability specification.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software lifecycle management is expected to follow IEC 62304, including planned releases, patch management, and version control. End-of-life procedures will include customer notification, data export support, and secure decommissioning of any hosted instances and associated personal data in line with DPDP obligations. Model retraining and updates are expected to be governed by the Algorithm Change Protocol filed with CDSCO.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: StrokeFast provides information that is intended to drive clinical management of patients in a critical healthcare situation. Acute stroke is a time-sensitive, life-threatening condition where diagnostic information directly influences urgent decisions such as activation of stroke pathways, thrombolysis eligibility, and thrombectomy referral. The device's output is expected to be used to prioritise or escalate cases, which the IMDRF SaMD framework characterises as 'drive clinical management' rather than 'inform' alone. Combining 'drive clinical management' with a 'critical' healthcare situation places the device at IMDRF Category III. The device is not positioned as a sole-decision diagnostic replacing the clinician (which would map to Category IV), but its influence on time-critical management justifies the Category III placement.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, software intended to diagnose or aid diagnosis of serious or critical conditions typically falls into Class C. The IMDRF Category III placement (drive clinical management × critical situation) aligns with CDSCO Class C, which corresponds to moderate-high risk devices regulated by the central licensing authority. A Class D call is generally reserved for software that solely and autonomously diagnoses or treats critical conditions without clinician oversight; StrokeFast is positioned as a clinician-in-the-loop adjunct, supporting a Class C call. This classification will be confirmed through a pre-submission interaction with CDSCO via SUGAM, and the sponsor is expected to seek written classification confirmation as part of early engagement.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Stroke is among the leading causes of death and disability in India, with treatment outcomes highly dependent on time-to-diagnosis and time-to-intervention. Non-contrast CT remains the first-line imaging modality in suspected acute stroke, but interpretation can be challenging in early infarct windows, particularly outside tertiary centres with on-site neuroradiology. AI-assisted triage has the potential to support faster identification of likely stroke cases, prioritise worklists, and accelerate referral for thrombolysis or thrombectomy where indicated. StrokeFast is intended to address this gap by providing a clinician-in-the-loop adjunct that supports timely escalation within stroke-ready pathways.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No clearly equivalent Indian predicate has been identified at this stage. Internationally, several AI-based stroke triage tools have received regulatory clearances in other jurisdictions (e.g., US FDA 510(k) clearances for large vessel occlusion notification software). These are expected to be referenced as comparator/benchmark devices to support the technical and clinical rationale, with the understanding that CDSCO does not operate a formal predicate-equivalence pathway analogous to 510(k). A literature and regulatory landscape review is expected to be included in the technical file.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The evidence strategy is expected to include: (i) analytical/standalone performance evaluation on a curated, demographically representative test set including Indian patient data, with metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, AUROC, and subgroup performance; (ii) a prospective clinical performance study at one or more lead Indian sites under Ethics Committee approval and CTRI registration, comparing device output against expert radiologist consensus as ground truth; (iii) usability evaluation per IEC 62366-1; and (iv) post-market real-world performance monitoring linked to the Algorithm Change Protocol. The clinical study protocol is expected to be discussed with CDSCO during pre-submission engagement.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to be filed as part of the submission, defining the scope of anticipated modifications to the AI/ML model that may be implemented under the protocol without a fresh licence amendment. Anticipated modifications may include retraining on additional Indian patient data, performance threshold tuning within pre-specified bounds, and expansion of supported scanner models, all within the originally cleared intended use. Modifications outside the PCCP scope (such as new indications, new modalities, or autonomous decision-making) are expected to require a separate submission.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The change protocol is expected to specify, for each anticipated modification type: the data management practices (data sources, curation, labelling, bias assessment); the retraining and revalidation methodology; pre-specified acceptance criteria for analytical and clinical performance (including non-inferiority thresholds and subgroup performance floors); verification and validation activities; documentation and version control practices under IEC 62304; and the internal governance and sign-off process before any updated model is released to the field.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: A real-world performance monitoring plan is expected to track key indicators including model output distribution, clinician override and feedback rates, false-positive and false-negative trends where ground truth becomes available, data drift indicators across deployment sites, and adverse event signals via the Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI). Pre-defined thresholds are expected to trigger investigation, model rollback, or formal CDSCO notification. Periodic safety and performance reports are anticipated to be submitted in line with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft expectations.

executive_summary.body:
StrokeFast is an artificial-intelligence-powered Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) that analyzes non-contrast CT brain scans to detect findings consistent with acute stroke. The product is intended to support clinical decision-making in emergency radiology and neurology settings, where time-to-diagnosis is a critical determinant of patient outcome. Based on the information provided and the IMDRF SaMD risk framework, StrokeFast is expected to be classified as a Class C SaMD under the Medical Devices Rules (MDR) 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO Draft Guidance on SaMD. This classification reflects the intersection of 'drive clinical management' information significance and a 'serious' healthcare situation, corresponding to IMDRF Category III.

The applicable regulatory pathway is the central licensing authority route under MDR 2017. Given the investigational stage of the product and the absence of a recognized Indian predicate, the anticipated sequence involves obtaining a test license under Form MD-12 to enable clinical investigation, followed by a full manufacturing or import license application via Form MD-9 (or Form MD-7 for domestic manufacturing), supported by a complete technical dossier including clinical performance evidence. An Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is expected to be required in accordance with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft guidance, given the AI/ML adaptive nature of the software.

At present, StrokeFast is assessed at a red readiness band with a Readiness Score of 1 out of 10. No quality management system, technical documentation, Ethics Committee approval, or CDSCO engagement has been detected. Three material gaps require immediate prioritization before any regulatory submission activity is initiated: establishing a quality management system aligned with ISO 13485 and IEC 62304; securing Ethics Committee approval and initiating a CTRI-registered clinical validation study; and opening pre-submission engagement with CDSCO via the SUGAM portal to confirm classification and ACP expectations. The realistic timeline to market authorization is estimated at 24 to 36 months from the current baseline, contingent on clinical site availability and CDSCO review timelines.
executive_summary.pathway: Central Licensing Authority pathway — MD-12 test license for clinical investigation, followed by MD-9 manufacturing/import license application supported by full technical dossier and Algorithm Change Protocol; SUGAM portal submission anticipated
headline_gaps: Establish an ISO 13485-aligned QMS and IEC 62304 software development lifecycle framework before any clinical or regulatory activity begins.
Obtain Ethics Committee (IRB) approval, register the clinical validation study on CTRI, and generate performance evidence for the CDSCO technical dossier.
Open a SUGAM portal account and initiate a CDSCO pre-submission meeting to confirm SaMD classification and Algorithm Change Protocol scope for the stroke-detection use case.

intended_use.indication: StrokeFast is intended as a decision-support tool for the automated analysis of non-contrast CT brain images to identify imaging features potentially consistent with acute ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke. The software is intended to assist qualified clinicians in prioritizing and triaging patients presenting with suspected acute cerebrovascular events. It is not intended to replace clinical judgment or serve as the sole basis for diagnosis or treatment decisions.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are licensed radiologists, neurologists, and emergency medicine physicians operating within a hospital or emergency care setting. The software is designed for use by clinically trained professionals who possess the knowledge to interpret AI-generated outputs in the context of a patient's full clinical presentation. It is not intended for use by patients, caregivers, or non-clinical personnel.
intended_use.use_environment: StrokeFast is intended for use in hospital-based environments including emergency departments, radiology reading rooms, and stroke care units where CT imaging infrastructure and PACS connectivity are available. The software may be deployed on-premise or via a cloud-connected interface, subject to applicable data security and ABDM integration requirements. Use in resource-limited or unmonitored settings outside a clinical facility is not within the intended scope.
intended_use.contraindications: StrokeFast is not intended for use as a standalone diagnostic device or as a replacement for radiologist interpretation. It is not indicated for pediatric populations, patients with prior significant intracranial surgery or hardware artifacts, or imaging acquired outside validated CT acquisition protocols. The software is not intended for use in screening of asymptomatic populations or for conditions other than acute cerebrovascular events. Outputs should not be used to initiate thrombolytic or interventional therapy without independent clinician review.

device_description.components_architecture: StrokeFast is a software-only medical device comprising three primary functional modules: (1) a DICOM image ingestion and preprocessing pipeline that receives non-contrast CT brain series from connected PACS or imaging systems; (2) a deep-learning inference engine, likely based on a convolutional neural network architecture trained on annotated CT stroke datasets, which generates probability scores and anatomical localization outputs for stroke-associated imaging findings; and (3) a results presentation layer that delivers structured output — including confidence scores, highlighted regions of interest, and triage flags — to the clinician-facing interface or integrates findings into the existing radiology workflow via HL7/FHIR messaging.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software applies supervised machine-learning algorithms, expected to include convolutional neural network models, to axial non-contrast CT brain image stacks. The model processes pixel-level feature representations across imaging slices to identify patterns associated with hyperdense vessel signs, early ischemic changes, parenchymal hemorrhage, or other CT correlates of acute stroke. The inference output is a probabilistic classification accompanied by spatial attention maps indicating regions contributing to the prediction. The system operates as a decision-support aid, with all clinical action remaining at the discretion of the supervising clinician.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, StrokeFast does not incorporate physical materials. Applicable software standards are expected to include IEC 62304 (Medical device software — Software life cycle processes), IEC 62366-1 (Usability engineering), ISO 14971 (Risk management), and ISO 27001 or equivalent for information security. DICOM conformance is expected for image input handling. Compliance with ISO 13485 is anticipated for the quality management system governing the software development and post-market surveillance lifecycle.
device_description.variants_accessories: At the time of this submission draft, StrokeFast is described as a single-configuration SaMD. If future variants are developed — such as models optimized for specific CT scanner manufacturers, contrast-enhanced protocols, or multi-modal inputs — each variant is expected to require a separate or amended classification and technical documentation review under MDR 2017. No physical accessories are associated with the current product scope. Third-party PACS and CT imaging hardware are considered predicate infrastructure, not accessories of this device.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: StrokeFast is a software product delivered via installation or cloud deployment; no physical disposal is applicable. The software lifecycle is governed by the IEC 62304-compliant software development and maintenance plan. End-of-life decommissioning involves revocation of access credentials, secure deletion of any locally cached patient data in accordance with DPDP Act 2023 obligations, and archival of device history records per MDR 2017 post-market requirements. Model version retirement and algorithm updates are governed by the Algorithm Change Protocol described in this dossier.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework, the risk category is determined by mapping information significance against the severity of the healthcare situation. StrokeFast provides outputs that are intended to drive clinical management — specifically, the triage and prioritization of patients with suspected acute stroke — placing it in the 'drive clinical management' column. Acute stroke represents a serious healthcare situation, defined by IMDRF as a condition that is not immediately life-threatening but where incorrect or delayed information may lead to serious injury or harm. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' and 'serious' healthcare situation maps to IMDRF Category III. While acute stroke carries time-critical life-threatening dimensions, the software's role as a decision-support aid rather than an autonomous treatment-initiating system supports Category III rather than Category IV.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under MDR 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO Draft Guidance on SaMD, IMDRF Category III SaMD is expected to correspond to CDSCO Class C. Class C devices are characterized as high-risk devices requiring central licensing authority review and a comprehensive technical dossier including clinical performance data. StrokeFast's classification as Class C is further supported by its AI/ML architecture — which introduces algorithm-specific risks including dataset bias, model drift, and opacity of inference — and its deployment in an acute emergency setting where erroneous outputs may adversely influence time-sensitive clinical decisions. The Class C designation is consistent with the treatment of comparable AI-based diagnostic imaging SaMD in analogous jurisdictions and aligns with the expectation that an Algorithm Change Protocol will be required as a condition of approval.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Acute stroke is among the leading causes of mortality and long-term disability in India, with an estimated incidence of 105–152 per 100,000 persons annually. Access to specialist neurological and neuroradiology expertise is highly uneven, with significant capacity gaps in tier-2 and tier-3 hospitals and emergency settings. Non-contrast CT brain imaging is the first-line modality for stroke evaluation due to its speed and wide availability; however, timely expert interpretation is frequently constrained by radiologist availability, particularly during off-hours. StrokeFast is intended to address this diagnostic bottleneck by providing automated, AI-assisted triage flagging of CT scans with stroke-consistent findings, enabling faster escalation and reducing time-to-treatment in emergency workflows.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No Indian-market CDSCO-approved predicate device for AI-based CT brain stroke detection has been identified at the time of this draft. Internationally, devices including Viz.ai (FDA 510(k) De Novo authorization), Aidoc Brain CT (FDA cleared), and RapidAI CT Stroke (FDA and CE marked) represent functionally comparable SaMD products in the same intended-use category. These predicates may be referenced to support clinical context, performance benchmarking, and risk characterization in the technical dossier; however, given the absence of a recognized Indian predicate, the CDSCO pathway is expected to follow a novel SaMD route requiring independent clinical performance validation in an Indian patient population.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy is expected to include a multi-site retrospective validation study using de-identified CT brain datasets with radiologist-confirmed ground truth labels, followed by a prospective observational clinical investigation at one or more ICMR-recognized hospital sites. The prospective study is expected to require Ethics Committee approval from an accredited Institutional Review Board and CTRI registration prior to initiation, in accordance with ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023. Primary performance endpoints are expected to include sensitivity, specificity, area under the ROC curve, and time-to-alert metrics benchmarked against standard-of-care reporting. The clinical investigation protocol and data package are anticipated to form a core component of the MD-9 or MD-7 technical dossier submitted to CDSCO.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is expected to be developed in accordance with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD Draft Guidance and aligned with international best practice from FDA's 2024 Marketing Submission Recommendations for AI/ML-enabled Devices. The PCCP is anticipated to define the scope of permissible algorithm updates — including model retraining with additional CT datasets, threshold recalibration, and preprocessing pipeline modifications — that may be implemented without triggering a full re-submission, provided pre-specified performance bounds are maintained. The PCCP is expected to specify the training data governance framework, performance acceptance criteria, and the conditions under which a change falls outside the pre-approved envelope and requires a formal variation application to CDSCO.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is expected to document: (1) the baseline model version approved at time of initial licensure, including architecture description, training dataset provenance, and validated performance metrics; (2) a change classification matrix distinguishing minor updates (e.g., bug fixes, UI changes) from moderate updates (e.g., retraining on expanded datasets) and major updates (e.g., new clinical indication, new input modality); (3) internal validation and performance regression testing requirements before deployment of any update; and (4) the notification and approval pathway for each change category under MDR 2017 post-market obligations. The ACP is expected to be a living document maintained under version control within the ISO 13485 QMS.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market performance monitoring is expected to include a structured real-world data collection program capturing model output distributions, clinician override rates, and adverse event or near-miss reports from deployed sites. A periodic performance review cadence — anticipated to be at minimum annual — is expected to compare real-world sensitivity and specificity against the approved performance benchmarks. Significant performance drift is expected to trigger the change classification process defined in the ACP. Data collection and incident reporting obligations are expected to align with MDR 2017 post-market surveillance requirements and DPDP Act 2023 data handling obligations, including de-identification of patient-level monitoring data.

executive_summary.body:
OncoPath Assist is an AI-enabled software-as-a-medical-device (SaMD) intended to assist pathologists in grading cancer from digitised histopathology slide images. Because the software analyses specimens derived from the human body to inform a diagnostic decision, it likely qualifies as an in-vitro diagnostic SaMD (IVD-SaMD) under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017 and the October 2025 CDSCO Draft Guidance on SaMD. The IMDRF risk framework places the device at Category III on the basis that it is intended to drive clinical management in a serious healthcare situation (oncology grading), which typically maps to CDSCO Class C and the Central Licensing Authority pathway.

The regulatory route anticipated for OncoPath Assist is a domestic manufacturing pathway via Form MD-7 to the Central Licensing Authority, preceded by a Test Licence under Form MD-12 to enable clinical performance evaluation of the IVD on Indian patient samples. Clinical validation is expected to be conducted in partnership with NABL-accredited pathology laboratories under an Ethics Committee approval aligned with the ICMR Ethical Guidelines for Application of AI in Biomedical Research and Healthcare (2023). Given the AI/ML nature of the algorithm, an Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is anticipated to be expected as part of the submission, in line with the October 2025 SaMD draft.

Readiness against this exposure is currently low. No ISO 13485 quality management system or IEC 62304 software lifecycle process has been established, no NABL laboratory partnership or clinical performance dataset is in place, and there has been no prior CDSCO engagement or SUGAM account creation. Foundational quality and evidence-generation work is expected to be initiated before a submission becomes viable. The estimated end-to-end timeline is 24–42 months, dominated by QMS build-out, prospective clinical performance evaluation, and central authority review.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — MD-7 application to CDSCO Central Licensing Authority, preceded by an MD-12 Test Licence for clinical performance evaluation of the IVD-SaMD
headline_gaps: Establish an ISO 13485 QMS and IEC 62304 software lifecycle process via a CDSCO-experienced consultant (6–9 month build).
Initiate a NABL-accredited pathology laboratory partnership and draft an Ethics Committee protocol aligned with ICMR AI guidelines before any clinical data collection.
Create a SUGAM portal account and seek a pre-submission consultation with CDSCO Central Licensing Authority to confirm the IVD-SaMD Class C hypothesis under the Oct 2025 SaMD draft.

intended_use.indication: OncoPath Assist is intended as a computer-assisted diagnostic aid that analyses digitised whole-slide images of histopathology specimens and outputs a suggested cancer grade to support pathologist interpretation. The software is intended for use as an adjunct to, and not a replacement for, definitive review by a qualified pathologist. The specific tumour types, staining protocols, and grading systems supported are expected to be specified in the labelling once analytical validation is complete.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended users are qualified anatomical pathologists and cytopathologists practising in NABL-accredited diagnostic laboratories, hospital-based pathology departments, and tertiary cancer care centres. The software is not intended for use by patients, general practitioners, or non-pathologist clinicians, and outputs are not intended to be communicated directly to patients without pathologist review and sign-off.
intended_use.use_environment: The product is intended for deployment in pathology laboratories equipped with digital slide scanners producing whole-slide images of validated quality, networked workstations meeting specified hardware requirements, and secure connectivity to the laboratory information system. Deployment may be on-premises or via a validated cloud environment compliant with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023. Use is expected to be confined to controlled clinical-laboratory settings; point-of-care or home use is out of scope.
intended_use.contraindications: OncoPath Assist is not intended for use on slide images outside the validated tumour types, stains, and scanner models declared in the labelling. It is not intended for use on intra-operative frozen sections, on cytology preparations not specifically validated, or as the sole basis for clinical decision-making. Use on image quality below specified thresholds, or for patient populations outside the validation cohort, is contraindicated.

device_description.components_architecture: OncoPath Assist is a standalone software medical device comprising: (i) an image ingestion module accepting whole-slide image formats from validated digital pathology scanners; (ii) a pre-processing and tissue-segmentation pipeline; (iii) a trained deep-learning inference engine that produces region-level features and a slide-level grade suggestion; (iv) a pathologist-facing user interface for review, override, and reporting; and (v) an audit-logging and model-versioning subsystem. Deployment may be on-premises or via a validated cloud environment, with secure interfaces to laboratory information systems where applicable.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The software applies a convolutional neural network trained on annotated histopathology images to generate a probabilistic suggestion of cancer grade for a given whole-slide image. The pathologist reviews regions of interest highlighted by the algorithm, may override the suggestion, and issues the final report. The output is advisory only and is intended to support, not substitute, expert interpretation. Model performance characteristics, including sensitivity, specificity, and confidence calibration, are expected to be characterised during analytical and clinical performance evaluation.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, no patient-contacting materials are involved. Development is expected to follow ISO 13485:2016 (QMS), IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), and ISO/IEC 27001 controls for information security. AI-specific lifecycle activities are anticipated to follow the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft and IMDRF SaMD guidance, with reference to relevant good machine learning practice principles.
device_description.variants_accessories: A single software variant is anticipated at initial submission. Validated digital slide scanners and viewing workstations are accessories specified by compatibility list rather than supplied by the manufacturer. Future variants supporting additional tumour types or stains are expected to be introduced through controlled change management under the QMS and, where applicable, the Algorithm Change Protocol.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: Software lifecycle activities — including release, deployment, post-market surveillance, complaint handling, and decommissioning — are expected to follow IEC 62304 and ISO 13485 procedures. Disposal involves secure deletion of software instances and patient-derived data in accordance with the DPDP Act 2023 retention and erasure obligations. No physical disposal hazards are anticipated.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: The output of OncoPath Assist — a suggested cancer grade derived from pathology imaging — is intended to inform the pathologist's diagnostic report, which in turn drives clinical management decisions including treatment intensity, surgical planning, and adjuvant therapy selection. Under the IMDRF SaMD: Possible Framework for Risk Categorization (N12), this corresponds to a significance of information of 'drive clinical management'. The healthcare situation is 'serious', given that cancer is a condition where misdiagnosis or mis-grading can lead to materially adverse patient outcomes, but is not typically immediately life-threatening at the point of grading. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' and 'serious' situates the device in IMDRF Category III. This is consistent with the treatment of AI-enabled diagnostic SaMD in the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the CDSCO Medical Devices Rules 2017, in-vitro diagnostic medical devices intended for the detection, diagnosis, or staging of cancer are typically classified as Class C, reflecting moderate-to-high individual risk and moderate public health risk. As an IVD-SaMD performing cancer grading from histopathology images, OncoPath Assist is expected to align with this Class C call. The classification is also consistent with the IMDRF Category III placement above and with the indicative classification approach in the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft for AI-enabled diagnostic software in serious clinical states. A Class C call routes the application to the Central Licensing Authority and brings with it expectations around clinical performance evaluation, ISO 13485 / IEC 62304 conformity, and an Algorithm Change Protocol. Final classification is expected to be confirmed through a CDSCO pre-submission consultation.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Accurate and reproducible cancer grading from histopathology slides is foundational to oncology treatment planning, yet inter-observer variability among pathologists is well documented in the literature, and India faces a structural shortage of specialist pathologists relative to its cancer burden. AI-assisted grading has the potential to improve consistency, reduce turnaround times, and extend access to specialist-grade interpretation in tier-2 and tier-3 settings, provided performance is robustly validated on representative Indian patient populations and the tool is positioned as an adjunct to pathologist judgement.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No clear Indian predicate IVD-SaMD for AI-based cancer grading from pathology slides has been identified at this stage. Internationally, several US FDA-cleared and CE-marked AI pathology tools exist for related indications (e.g. prostate cancer detection and grading), and these may serve as comparative references during analytical and clinical performance evaluation, though they are not expected to substitute for India-specific clinical evidence. The absence of an Indian predicate reinforces an investigational pathway and a fuller clinical performance evaluation under MD-12 / MD-23.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy is expected to comprise: (i) analytical performance characterisation on a curated, multi-scanner image dataset; (ii) a retrospective concordance study against expert pathologist consensus on de-identified Indian histopathology cases sourced from NABL-accredited laboratories; and (iii) a prospective clinical performance evaluation under an MD-12 Test Licence at multiple Indian sites, with Ethics Committee approval aligned to the ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare (2023). Endpoints are anticipated to include sensitivity, specificity, agreement with expert consensus, and pathologist workflow impact.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan is anticipated to be submitted as part of the dossier, scoping the categories of model updates the manufacturer intends to deploy post-market without requiring fresh approval. Likely in-scope changes include retraining on expanded Indian patient datasets, recalibration for new validated scanner models, and threshold tuning informed by post-market performance monitoring. Out-of-scope changes — such as new tumour types, new grading systems, or shifts in intended use — are expected to be handled through fresh regulatory submissions. The PCCP is expected to be aligned with the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD draft and with international good machine learning practice principles.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol is expected to specify, for each in-scope change category: the data requirements (sample size, demographic representativeness, scanner diversity), the analytical and clinical re-validation tests to be performed, the acceptance criteria (e.g. non-inferiority bounds on sensitivity and specificity), the verification and documentation steps under IEC 62304 and ISO 13485, and the internal sign-off pathway before deployment. Version control, model traceability, and audit logging are expected to be embedded in the QMS so that every deployed model version is reproducible and inspectable.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring is anticipated to include: tracking of pathologist override rates and disagreement patterns; periodic concordance re-checks against expert consensus on sampled cases; drift detection across scanner models, staining batches, and site-level case mixes; complaint and adverse-event capture under MDR 2017 vigilance obligations; and a defined cadence of performance reports to internal governance and, where applicable, to CDSCO. Triggers for unscheduled re-validation or market action — such as performance falling below pre-specified thresholds — are expected to be defined in the ACP.

executive_summary.body:
OncoPath Assist is an artificial-intelligence-powered Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) designed to suggest cancer grading from digitised pathology slide images. Based on published CDSCO and IMDRF guidance, the product likely qualifies as an In-Vitro Diagnostic SaMD (IVD-SaMD) of Class C under the Medical Devices Rules 2017, placing it among the higher-risk categories in the Indian regulatory landscape. The classification is anchored in the product's intended function: providing diagnostic information — specifically a cancer grade — that is expected to directly influence clinical management decisions in a serious disease state.

The applicable regulatory framework draws from the Medical Devices Rules 2017, the CDSCO October 2025 Draft Guidance on SaMD, and aligned IMDRF SaMD principles. The central licensing authority pathway is the anticipated route, with clinical performance evaluation, an Algorithm Change Protocol, and a compliant quality management system constituting the foundational prerequisites. DPDP Act 2023 compliance for sensitive health data and ICMR Ethics Committee approval for clinical validation studies are additionally expected obligations.

At present, readiness is critically low across all material dimensions. No ISO 13485-compliant quality management system or IEC 62304 software lifecycle documentation has been detected. No clinical validation data exists, and no partnership with a NABL-accredited pathology laboratory has been identified. The company has no documented prior engagement with CDSCO and no SUGAM account on record. These gaps collectively place the product at an early investigational stage, with a realistic approval timeline of 24 to 42 months contingent on immediate foundational action.

The three most urgent priorities are: establishing a compliant QMS under ISO 13485 and IEC 62304; securing a NABL-accredited laboratory partner to anchor the clinical performance evaluation dataset; and initiating CDSCO pre-submission engagement via the SUGAM portal to confirm the Class C IVD-SaMD pathway under the evolving SaMD draft guidance. Founders should treat these as parallel workstreams rather than sequential steps to avoid compounding timeline risk.
executive_summary.pathway: Domestic manufacturing — Central Licensing Authority pathway under Medical Devices Rules 2017; anticipated forms include MD-12 (test licence for clinical investigation), MD-9 (import/manufacturing licence as applicable), and MD-23 (IVD clinical performance evaluation); pathway subject to finalisation of the October 2025 CDSCO SaMD Draft Guidance
headline_gaps: Initiate ISO 13485 QMS gap assessment and IEC 62304 software lifecycle documentation immediately — both are likely prerequisites before any CDSCO submission is viable.

Identify and contract a NABL-accredited pathology laboratory for clinical performance evaluation, and obtain Ethics Committee approval aligned with ICMR AI Healthcare Guidelines 2023 before collecting any patient data.

Create a SUGAM portal account and schedule a pre-submission consultation with the CDSCO Central Licensing Authority to confirm IVD-SaMD Class C classification and current SaMD draft pathway requirements.

intended_use.indication: OncoPath Assist is intended to analyse digitised whole-slide pathology images and generate a suggested cancer grade to support trained pathologists in the assessment of histopathological specimens. The device is intended as a decision-support aid in oncology diagnostic workflows and is not intended to replace the independent clinical judgement of a licensed pathologist. The output is expected to serve as one input among multiple data sources considered during a clinical diagnosis.
intended_use.intended_user: The intended primary users are licensed and trained pathologists and histopathology specialists operating within accredited diagnostic laboratories or hospital-based pathology departments. Users are expected to possess formal training in histopathology, familiarity with digital pathology platforms, and the clinical expertise necessary to critically evaluate AI-generated grading suggestions in the context of the full clinical picture. The device is not intended for use by non-specialist clinical or administrative personnel.
intended_use.use_environment: OncoPath Assist is intended for use in clinical laboratory environments, including NABL-accredited diagnostic laboratories, hospital pathology departments, and digital pathology centres equipped with whole-slide imaging systems and compliant computing infrastructure. The device operates as software interfacing with digital slide scanners and laboratory information systems. Use in uncontrolled or non-clinical settings is outside the intended scope of this device.
intended_use.contraindications: OncoPath Assist is not intended for use as a standalone diagnostic tool or as a replacement for pathologist review. The device is not indicated for use with image types other than digitised histopathological whole-slide images of the tissue types included in the validated dataset. Use outside the validated cancer types, tissue origins, staining protocols, or scanner hardware configurations specified in the device's Instructions for Use is outside the intended use. The device is not intended for paediatric oncology applications unless separately validated.

device_description.components_architecture: OncoPath Assist is a software-only medical device comprising: (1) a digital image ingestion module that accepts whole-slide image files in standard formats from compatible slide scanners; (2) a pre-processing pipeline performing image normalisation, tiling, and quality-gating; (3) a trained deep-learning inference engine executing cancer grade classification; (4) a structured output and reporting module that presents grading suggestions and confidence indicators to the reviewing pathologist; and (5) an audit and logging subsystem maintaining records of all analyses for traceability. The device is deployed as a server-based application accessible via a web interface within the institution's secure network environment.
device_description.principle_of_operation: The device applies a convolutional neural network trained on annotated histopathological whole-slide images to identify morphological patterns associated with established cancer grading schemas. Input images are pre-processed and passed through the inference engine, which generates a probabilistic grading suggestion corresponding to defined grade categories. The output is presented alongside a confidence metric and tile-level heatmap overlays intended to direct the pathologist's attention to regions of interest. The system does not make a final diagnostic determination; it augments the pathologist's review by surfacing algorithmically identified patterns for human verification.
device_description.materials_standards: As a software-only device, OncoPath Assist does not incorporate physical materials. Applicable standards expected to govern the device include IEC 62304 (medical device software lifecycle processes), IEC 62366-1 (usability engineering), ISO 14971 (risk management), ISO 13485 (quality management systems), and relevant IMDRF guidance documents on SaMD and AI/ML-based SaMD. Data security and privacy controls are expected to align with ISO 27001 principles and the requirements of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023.
device_description.variants_accessories: At the time of this submission, OncoPath Assist is presented as a single software configuration. Future variants addressing additional cancer types, tissue origins, or staining protocols would be subject to separate validation studies and, depending on the nature and significance of the change, may require regulatory notification or re-submission under the applicable Algorithm Change Protocol. The device is compatible with a defined list of whole-slide imaging scanners specified in the Instructions for Use; these scanners are not accessories of the device and are regulated independently.
device_description.lifecycle_disposal: OncoPath Assist is a software product with no physical disposal obligations. The device lifecycle is governed by the IEC 62304 software maintenance and decommissioning procedures defined in the Quality Management System. Retirement of a deployed instance requires secure deletion of all locally stored patient data in accordance with the DPDP Act 2023 data retention and erasure obligations. Version-controlled release and retirement records are maintained in the device master record throughout the product lifecycle.

risk_classification.imdrf_rationale: Under the IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework, risk category is determined by the significance of information provided by the SaMD and the healthcare situation to which it is applied. OncoPath Assist provides a cancer grade suggestion that is directly intended to inform the pathologist's diagnostic conclusion — this constitutes information intended to 'drive clinical management' rather than merely inform or triage. The healthcare situation is 'serious': cancer is a condition that, without accurate and timely diagnosis, is liable to cause serious deterioration or long-term morbidity. The intersection of 'drive clinical management' and 'serious' anchors the device at IMDRF Category III, reflecting a high-risk profile that warrants rigorous pre-market clinical performance evaluation and robust post-market surveillance.
risk_classification.cdsco_rationale: Under the Medical Devices Rules 2017 and aligned CDSCO SaMD draft guidance published in October 2025, an IVD-SaMD at IMDRF Category III is expected to correspond to Class C in the Indian classification scheme. Class C encompasses active devices and in-vitro diagnostic devices intended to provide information for diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, or treatment of life-threatening conditions. OncoPath Assist meets this criterion directly: it provides AI-generated cancer grading information in the context of a serious, potentially life-threatening disease. The central licensing authority pathway is consequently the anticipated route, with clinical performance evaluation data and a compliant QMS likely required as prerequisites to any market authorisation submission.

clinical_context.clinical_need: Cancer remains among the leading causes of mortality in India, with histopathological grading serving as a cornerstone of oncology treatment planning. Manual grading of pathology slides is highly dependent on pathologist expertise, is subject to inter-observer variability, and faces a significant workforce shortage in specialist pathology across Tier 2 and Tier 3 centres. AI-assisted grading tools have the potential to reduce diagnostic variability, flag high-grade lesions for urgent review, and extend the reach of specialist-level analysis to under-resourced diagnostic settings. OncoPath Assist is positioned to address this unmet need by providing a consistent, auditable, algorithm-generated grading suggestion that pathologists may use to calibrate and validate their own assessments.
clinical_context.predicate_devices: No confirmed Indian CDSCO-approved predicate for an AI-powered histopathology cancer grading SaMD has been identified at the time of this submission, which anchors the device on the novel/investigational regulatory track. Internationally, several AI-assisted digital pathology tools have received regulatory clearance from the US FDA (e.g., Paige Prostate, Ibex Galen) and CE marking in the EU, which may serve as reference points for clinical performance benchmarking and evidence generation methodology, though these do not constitute Indian predicates for CDSCO purposes. The absence of a domestic predicate is expected to result in a more extensive pre-market clinical evidence requirement.
clinical_context.evidence_plan: The clinical evidence strategy for OncoPath Assist is expected to comprise: (1) an analytical performance evaluation establishing sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value against pathologist-adjudicated ground truth across validated cancer types and tissue origins; (2) a reader study or retrospective clinical performance evaluation conducted in partnership with at least one NABL-accredited pathology laboratory using ethically approved, de-identified Indian patient specimens; (3) prospective clinical investigation under MD-12 test licence if required by CDSCO; and (4) post-market clinical follow-up integrated with the Algorithm Change Protocol. All clinical data collection is expected to require prior Ethics Committee approval aligned with ICMR Ethical Guidelines for AI in Healthcare 2023.

algorithm_change_protocol.pccp: A Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) is recommended for OncoPath Assist given its adaptive AI/ML inference engine. The PCCP is expected to define in advance the categories of permissible model updates — including retraining on expanded datasets, recalibration of confidence thresholds, and extension to additional cancer subtypes — and the corresponding performance benchmarks that must be met before deployment. Changes within the PCCP scope that do not degrade clinical performance beyond pre-specified limits may be deployable without full re-submission, subject to CDSCO confirmation under the October 2025 SaMD draft pathway. All PCCP-governed changes are expected to be documented in the device master record and notified to the Central Licensing Authority per applicable post-market obligations.
algorithm_change_protocol.change_protocol: The Algorithm Change Protocol (ACP) is expected to establish a tiered classification of model changes: (i) minor changes, such as bug fixes or inference latency optimisations with no impact on grading output, managed through standard software change control under IEC 62304 without regulatory notification; (ii) moderate changes, such as dataset expansion within already-validated cancer types, requiring internal validation against pre-specified performance thresholds and documentation in the post-market surveillance record; and (iii) major changes, including extension to new cancer types, new tissue origins, or architectural model modifications, requiring prospective clinical performance evaluation and likely triggering a CDSCO variation submission. All changes are subject to a risk assessment under ISO 14971 prior to deployment.
algorithm_change_protocol.real_world_monitoring: Post-market real-world performance monitoring is expected to be conducted through a structured programme capturing: pathologist concordance rates between OncoPath Assist suggestions and final diagnostic conclusions; cases where the AI suggestion was overridden and the documented clinical rationale; adverse event and near-miss reporting aligned with MDR 2017 vigilance obligations; and periodic statistical drift analysis comparing live model output distributions against the validated baseline. Monitoring data is expected to feed into a scheduled post-market surveillance report and trigger protocol-defined model review cycles. Patient data processed for monitoring purposes is expected to comply with DPDP Act 2023 data minimisation and purpose-limitation requirements.
